notes: Ruhamah = "Having obtained mercy," or "those who have obtained mercy"
your mother = the nation Israel (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
wilderness = The captivity (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
mercy (Heb. racham) to love, love deeply, have mercy, be compassionate, have tender affection, have compassion
whoredoms (Heb. zanuwn) adultery, fornication, prostitution from zanah "play the harlot"

The image of prostitution was commonly used in the Old Testament to depict extreme apostasy not only from religion but from all that is godly and good (Isa. 1:21; 23:16; Jer. 2:20-31; 13:27; Ezek. 16:15, 28-29; Hosea 2:5; Nahum 3:4). (Donald W. Parry and Jay A. Parry, Understanding the Book of Revelation, p.219)
5-9 bread, corn, wool, and jewels = Worldly values and treasures (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
lovers = The priests, priestesses, and idols of the Canaanite temples or, in the larger sense, any person
one loves more than God (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
wall (Heb. gader) fence or wall from the root gadar "to wall up, wall off, close off"
path (Heb. nathiyb) pathway from from an unused root meaning to tramp
thorns (Heb. ciyr) caldron from a primitive root meaning to boil up
overtake (Heb. nasag) to reach, overtake, take hold upon
9-10 her nakedness and her lewdness = Israel's sin (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
lewdness (Heb. nabluwth) immodesty, shamelessness from nabal "foolishness"
deliver (Heb. natsal) to snatch away, deliver, rescue, save
11-14 allure her = Jehovah still cares for her and will try to win her back. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
Valley of Achor, a rich valley north of Jericho, near Gilgal = The Lord will restore her to great blessings. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
Ishi (Hebrew for "my husband") and Baali (Hebrew for "my master") = Eventually Israel will accept God as her Lord and her true husband. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)

As in the beginning, the whole face of the earth will be a delightful garden, with the major land surfaces joined together instead of separated by oceans. (See D&C 133:23-24.) The transfiguration of the earth will be so radical and complete, that it will be "a new earth." (Isa. 65:17.) Animals of the earth will live together in harmony as the natural cycle of predator and prey will be broken: "In that day the enmity . . . of beasts, yea, the enmity of all flesh, shall cease from before my face." (D&C 101:26.) Instead of animals eating animals, they will eat straw and other natural by-products of plant life. (See Isa. 11:6-7; 65:25; Hosea 2:18.) Thus, a harmonious relationship will exist on earth between nature, animals, and humans. (Victor L. Ludlow, Principles and Practices of the Restored Gospel, p.629)

The Lord further explained to Hosea concerning the millennial state of animals: "And in that day will I make a covenant for them with the beasts of the field, and with the fowls of heaven, and with the creeping things of the ground: and I will break the bow and the sword and the battle out of the earth, and will make them to lie down safely." (Hosea 2:18.) (Gerald E. Jones, "The Gospel and Animals," Ensign, Aug. 1972, 62)

quotes.text: The Lord further explained to Hosea concerning the millennial state of animals: "And in that day will I make a covenant for them with the beasts of the field, and with the fowls of heaven, and with the creeping things of the ground: and I will break the bow and the sword and the battle out of the earth, and will make them to lie down safely." (Hosea 2:18.)
quotes.attr: Gerald E. Jones, "The Gospel and Animals," Ensign, Aug. 1972, 62

notes: Importantly, Hosea was the first of the Old Testament prophets to correlate the covenant of marriage and the Lord's covenant with Israel, a characteristic whose implications have been spelled out by Abraham Heschel. Harlotry, for the Lord, expressed the antithesis of his covenant. In his anger, he asserted that "the spirit of whoredoms hath caused [Israel] to err, and they have gone a whoring from under their God. . . . Therefore your daughters shall commit who-redom, and your spouses shall commit adultery" (Hosea 4:12-13). While the imagery is not enticing, the point is abundantly clear: straying from the covenant of the Lord is tantamount to abandoning one's marriage covenant, deserting one's spouse for a harlot. One additional component is formed by the concept that coming to "know the Lord" is analogous to coming to know one's spouse in a union that is born in a covenant and proven over time with fidelity, love, and mutual trust. Once again, harlotry constituted the opposite. (S. Kent Brown in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 4: 1 Kings to Malachi [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1993], 65.)

19-20 betroth thee unto me forever = The fulness of the new and everlasting covenant restored to Israel in the latter days and the eternal blessings that will result from Israel's faithful marriage to Jehovah. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)

Jezreel (Hebrew for "God shall sow") = The down-trodden and poor Israel. Like the Jezreel Valley, they have great potential and will be resown and made fruitful by the Lord.. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 106)
